text: The surgical procedure is performed using four trocars, with the patient positioned on their left side.
The surgeon identifies the previous ileocolic anastomosis and performs a peritumoral injection of two milliliters of indocyanin green, which is visible as a green dye around the tumor site.
The surgeon conducts a lateral to medial approach due to the absence of the heliocolic pedicle, maneuvering instruments to access the surgical site.
The surgeon sections the lateral lesion and identifies the main landmarks for the heliocolic resection, including the duodenum and the right ureter.
Twenty-five minutes after the indocyanin green injection, the surgeon verifies the medial limit of resection, observing the dye's distribution to confirm the boundaries of the surgical area.
The surgeon identifies the superior mesenteric vein and an ileal branch during the procedure, with these structures visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates a segment of the small intestine approximately fifteen centimeters from the previously identified ileocolic anastomosis, preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon performs a mesocolic excision, following the superior mesenteric vein in a caudal to cranial direction, progressing towards the pancreatic head.
The surgeon uses fluorescence guidance to identify and remove fluorescent lymphatic tissue during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the middle colic vessels that were preserved during the previous right colectomy, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs the ileocolic anastomosis using a stapler, following the verification of vascularization of the two intestinal stumps with an intraoperative intravenous injection of indocyanine green (ICG).
The surgeon verifies hemostasis on the intraluminal side of the ileocolic anastomosis and sutures the service bridge using a V-lock suture.
